metadata.title: Extending monitoring methods to textual data: a research agenda

abstract:
Textual data has become increasingly common in business analytic data sets. While concept-based text mining offers a method of extracting meaningful information from text data, methods for monitoring of customer perceptions of business processes and products that are discussed in customer-generated documents are not immediately available. We explore the results of two text-mining algorithms and review issues observed in the data that affect uploading the results onto a newly proposed methodological monitoring platform analogous to statistical process control charts. Finally, we discuss several topics for future research in text mining.

Big Data is a relatively recent term that describes the challenge, experienced by many businesses, to handle data of unprecedented volume, variety, and velocity (Zikopoulos et al. 2012, p. 5). While much of big data is structured transactional data, ever-increasing volumes are documents, email, weblogs, social media, and other sources of unstructured natural language text data (Russom 2011;Intel 2012;Allen et al. 2011). paragraphs: present, and potential future customers. Frequently, these customers express opinions on services or products in these communications. This data is the voice of the customer and holds critical information that the competitive enterprise could leverage if appropriate methods were available.

A firm can easily begin collecting unstructured customer input by asking questions and providing space for the customer to type in a response on web pages, in such places as account registration forms, purchase orders, and product reviews. A firm can also capture and hold all customer email creating another source of data for analysis. Many social media sources also provide application programming interfaces (API) that allow the firm to collect data by searching the provider's content. Given these outlets, tremendous amounts of customeroriginated unstructured data are available, and the ability to collect data is limited only by the creativity of the firm to pose questions to the target population.

While few would argue about the importance and the rich information provided by unstructured text data, approaches for its analysis remain a largely unresolved problem. Similar to NVivo and MaxQDA (Franzosi et al. 2012), a semi-automatic approach that, until recently, was considered a de facto industry standard, involves a manual analysis step, where sampled documents are read to identify important key words, and an automated step, where ad-hoc queries would track the presence of those key words in the customer feedback data stream. The main problem with these approaches is that their manual steps generate considerable costs and their automated steps are prone to generating biased results.

Bias results can occur during many existing qualitative data analytic methods because during the manual reading the key terms that are identified are selected based on the researcher's perception of importance. Unlike the typical applications for software based qualitative tools such as NVivo and MaxQDA where the researcher can repeatedly visit a group of texts, high volumes and high velocity data characterize the big data environment of business. Repeated analysis sessions are not an option. One suggestion for alleviating bias is the use of multiple analytic methods; a process called triangulation (Leech and Onwuegbuzie 2008;Patton 1999). However, triangulation introduces a new problem because results might not produce a convergent solution (Poortman and Schildkamp 2012). In other instances, to prevent bias during manual qualitative analysis, the analysis is conducted independently by the key researcher and a nonaffiliated individual (Merlo et al. 2013).

These recommendations come from research disciplines that operate in relatively low data volumes. Due to the move to automation that began in earnest in the 1980s, business now commonly maintain terabit size data bases with millions of observations. Manual and semiautomated analysis methods are not well suited to such an environment and triangulation or independent validation is not practical. In business, the use of nonaffiliated individuals is also typically not acceptable for other reasons. The qualitative data a business possesses comes from past and present customers and reveals strengths and weaknesses in the enterprise. Disclosure of such information is potentially problematic in a competitive market. Finally, analysis by hand including using such semi-automatic methods with software such as Max-QDA and NVivo is time consuming and would be prohibitively expensive with teradata. Analyzing twice either by triangulation or by a nonaffiliated independent analyst is twice as expensive.

Fortunately, alternative statistical and probabilistic methods for automated concept extraction from unstructured data have emerged. Modern text mining methods have the ability to extract what Swanborn (1996) refers to as informative knowledge from a corpus by mathematical means. Such methods are typically categorized under two practically equivalent umbrella terms: text mining, which includes the uncovering of underlying themes or concepts contained in large document collections (SAS 2012, p. 1), and text analytics, which includes the identification of core concepts, sentiments and trends in unstructured data, and their use in decision making support (IBM 2010, p. 3). Automated concept extraction methods alleviate the difficulties associated with unstructured data analysis by offering dramatic efficiency improvements over manual analysis, as well as significant effectiveness improvements over simple automated techniques.

In recent years text mining has focused on providing a mechanism to analyze textual data and uncover hidden concepts and little effort was devoted to developing convenient methods for the temporal monitoring of these concepts, or topics. Many of the topics extracted from a corpus by a text mining method describe the activities of, or are the result of, a business process. In the backdrop of a changing environment, that process can result in failing to satisfy customers, and will continue to fail, until issues raised by the customers are addressed by management. The researcher or analyst needs appropriate tools for monitoring and assessing the severity of each of these ongoing topics.

In this paper, our objective is to extend existing text mining methods by adding methods for monitoring ongoing discussion topics. We focus our efforts on business corpora that have time-series components. That is, corpora where customer expressions are composed in an ongoing manner in response to either a question posed by the business entity, a third party entity such as Consumer Reports, or postings on a social media outlet. These customer responses provide opinions on business process effectiveness or product fit, and valuable insight to the process manager. More specifically, customers focus primarily on a set of topics associated with the quality of service, timeliness of shipping, price, and product availability. These topics are either service quality (SERVQUAL), logistics service quality (LSQ), or customer satisfaction related items. It is critical for any institution to monitor the status of these topics.

The ability to monitor these ongoing discussion topics provides managers with information about the status of the enterprise with regard to the topic. The data monitoring also provides input for managing improvement programs. Additionally, a monitoring mechanism also allows managers to determine the success of process improvement initiatives. Text mining originated in the computer science (CS) and information retrieval (IR) disciplines (Deerwester et al. 1990;Blei et al. 2003). In the IR context, the data set is even larger than that of business e.g. the internet. In IR, the focus is on getting the best single solution to the corpus so that users can efficiently retrieve documents pertinent to a list of key terms.

In the literature, the monitoring of topics extracted by text-mining is limited to Lo (2008) as well as Ashton and Evangelopoulos (2012). Lo (2008) uses a method of extracting topics from a corpus called support vector machine (SVM). After the SVM extracts the various topics, they are classified according to subject. Then all customer submissions that are classified as either complaints or problem related are combined into a single data stream that is monitored by a p-chart. Ashton and Evangelopoulos (2012) illustrate the application of text-mined data to the exponentially weighted moving average (EWMA) chart. This latter work maintains the different topics extracted as separate solutions and monitors them individually. Such separate monitoring has the advantage of providing feedback about each specific topic.

Qualitative data offers the richest information source available for any data collection effort. Its analysis and monitoring, however, are problematic. This work is an important step in addressing these issues and can serve as a foundation for the development of techniques for monitoring topics of interest found in text data.

sections.paper_id: Ashton et al. - 2014 - Extending monitoring methods to textual data a research agenda
paragraphs: Text mining methods fall into one of two broad classes: context word models and context region models. Context word models analyze the data by computing frequencies of wordword combinations found in a text collection. These methods view the text through an n-word wide window and build a matrix out of the data by counting the frequency of occurrence for each word-word combination. Context word models are very efficient at determining word substitutions and are applied extensively in cognitive sciences.

Context region models build a matrix by counting the frequency of words that appear in each region. The region can be taken as a sentence, paragraph, email, or whole document depending on how the researcher/analyst chooses to approach the data. Independent of how the data is partitioned into regions, we generally refer to these regions as documents. In most business settings, we generally allow the entire customer expression to be recorded as a single document. In this research, we are primarily interested in the context region class of text mining methods, which constitute the predominant models in business applications.

The context region class of text mining methods consists of four subfamilies of algorithms. These include latent semantic analysis (LSA), (Deerwester et al. 1990), non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) (Lee and Seung 1999), probabilistic latent semantic indexing (pLSI) (Hofmann 1999), and latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) (Blei et al. 2003).

As a first attempt at describing a corpus in probabilistic terms, pLSI provided a partial probabilistic model (Blei et al. 2003). Since it is now generally viewed as a precursor to LDA, pLSI is not considered further in this work. While developed separately, NMF and pLSI were shown to provide an analytical solution to the same optimization problem (Gaussier and Goutte 2005;Ding et al. 2008). The NMF community argues that the semantic space of a corpus involves an absolute origin and that recentering data causes a loss of information about that origin (Paatero and Tapper 1994). However, in text mining we routinely encounter high frequency words that will dominate a corpus yet contribute little unique information value. The effect is analogous to the astronomical problem where a high-density mass blocks the observation of nearby lighter bodies. A text mining method has to adjust for this distribution anomaly because the lighter bodied (lower frequency) terms convey the most information. Centering the data was repeatedly shown effective at countering these distribution imbalances when combined with a distribution-weighting scheme. As a consequence, ongoing research has yet to observe NMF performing consistently on a par with LSA and LDA. As a result of these issues, this research is limited in focus to LSA and LDA.

LSA was the first subfamily of context region text mining models. The development of LSA was motivated by the need for a method of retrieving information based on conceptual content instead of just words that are present in a document (Deerwester et al. 1990). LSA infers indirect similarity of meaning and captures the similarities of words so well that the model improves at the approximate rate of natural learning for young children (Laundauer and Dumais 1997). Psychologically speaking, LSA was described as a theory of learning, memory, and knowledge capable of exhibiting humanlike generalization (Laundauer and Dumais 1997).

Latent Dirichlet allocation was the first analysis method of what has become known as the topic models subfamily of text mining methods. There are many variations to the original LDA algorithm now available. Most of these variations are task specific; therefore, in this study we elect to stay with the original algorithm.

The extant literature has not clarified which of these two algorithms is best for any given task. Comparative studies between LSA and LDA are extremely limited. In recent testing in cognitive research, the two methods were found to perform similarly

sections.paper_id: Ashton et al. - 2014 - Extending monitoring methods to textual data a research agenda
paragraphs: Most text-mining practitioners apply a series of preprocessing steps to the corpus before the main mathematical algorithm is executed. The first of these steps eliminates high frequency words. In most circles, stoplists of low information, high frequency words such as the conjugates, interjections, and prepositions, plus function specific words are removed from the corpus. Frequently, other items are removed from the corpus: for example, words with less than three letters, words occurring with less than some predetermined frequency, and occasionally numbers.

After stoplist items are removed, words are stemmed back to their roots removing suffixes. Variants of a basic word redistribute the probability of one word across the variations resulting in a distorted word distribution that underestimates the true frequency of an underlying concept. The Porter (1980) and the Snowball stemmer (Porter 2001) are common stemming methods.

With the vocabulary reduced by a stoplist and stemmed to its roots, a term-by-document frequency matrix is built by counting the number of occurrence of each term in each document. The cells of this frequency matrix, referred to as the X matrix, hold the frequency counts of each term found in each document of the corpus. Once the X matrix is generated, the main analytic algorithm can be applied.

paragraphs: Latent semantic analysis begins by weighting the term frequencies of the X matrix. The objective of weighting the frequencies is to redistribute the probabilities of terms. Redistributing the probabilities elevates the probability of terms that appear in limited numbers of document with higher frequency and reduces the probability of terms that appear in many documents. There is a number of term weighting schemes available. Term frequency by inverse document frequency and log-entropy based transformations are two of the leading transformations used with business corpora (Evangelopoulos et al. 2012). After the X matrix is weighted, it is decomposed by singular value decomposition (SVD) as illustrated by Fig. 1 into three component matrices given by

The k dimensions associated with the various matrices relate to the topics discovered in the corpus by LSA. Topics, also called factors in the language of principal components analysis and principal factor analysis, are essentially clusters in vector space that explain the greatest portion of variance in the data.

Once the X matrix is reduced to its component matrices, there are two loading matrices, L T and L D that are produced. Using the orthonormality property V T V = I, where I is a k × k identity matrix, we obtain

The L T matrix identifies the terms that are highly correlated to the various topics. The L T matrix also gives us term loadings from which we can assess how strongly a term correlates to a particular topic. The L T matrix is a t × k matrix where each column represents a different discussion topic that LSA discovers in the original corpus. The top five-to-ten terms in each topic are typically sufficient in defining or describing the topics.

With the topics defined, we next examine how documents correlate to the various topics. This is accomplished with the help of factor loadings. To obtain document factor loadings, we use the orthonormality property U T U = I, where I is the k × k identity matrix, and S = S T . Post-multiplying both sides by U t×k gives

The L D matrix is a d ×k document loadings matrix that associates the corpus documents with specific discussion topics. Documents identified in column k of the L D matrix are associated with the topic described by column k of the L T matrix. When a document is highly correlated with the kth discussion topic, it will have a high loading level listed in the respective column k of the L D matrix. It will also use many of the terms that are highly correlated to topic k in the L T matrix. The document then discusses topic k and includes in its text the words that define topic k.

Documents with very low loading values are usually only peripherally associated with a discussion topic because they happen to use a limited number of the terms that define the topic. It has become common practice to suppress loading values below some document threshold θ D and not consider them part of the final solution. While this practice will occasionally result in removing a valid document from a discussion topic, it primarily reduces the injection of noise into the solution.

Thresholds θ D are determined by listing all document loadings in rank order. For a corpus with n documents, this listing will be n × k = j in length. We then select the loading level of the j/k document as θ D . Expressed another way, with documents listed in rank order of loading level, the loading of the nth document is taken as θ D . All documents with a loading less than θ D are suppressed (loadings set to zero) resulting in a list with n observations generated from a corpus of n documents. At this point, the data is ready for any subsequent analytic technique desired.

paragraphs: LDA proposes the most elegant theory of a corpus. LDA defines a corpus from the bottom up by considering that it is a vector consisting of a set of V discrete word unit's w. A document is denoted w and consists of N words denoted w = {w 1 , w 2 , . . .,w N }. The corpus then consists of M documents that are denoted D = {w 1 , w 2 , . . .,w M }. The theory continues stating that a corpus is composed of a mix of z n topics that approximately follow the Multinomial(θ) distribution where θ is a mixture parameter that follows a Dirichlet(α). The parameter α is a k-vector of components whos values are α i > 0. Word choice w n for each topic follow a multinomial probability distribution p(w n |z n , β). The β is a k × V word probability matrix where β i j = p(w j = 1|z i = 1) (Blei et al. 2003). With the corpus structure defined, the key inferential problem is reduced to computing the posterior distribution following
However, that distribution is mathematically intractable as a probabilistic model (Blei et al. 2003). LDA then considers the data as characterized by the variational distribution

After finding the optimal values for parameters γ and φ for each document in the corpus, the resulting lower bound for the log likelihood is maximized for the parameter β. This is referred to as the M-step. Holding φ and γ to the values found during variational inference, estimates are obtained by maximizing the lower bound. The E and M steps are repeated until the lower bound of the log likelihood converges. The β is the Dirichlet parameter for the word-topic distribution. The β is recovered from
In this work, we are only concerned with the β and γ parameter (Blei et al. 2003).

Because of the optimization operations, LDA, under repetitive trials, produces results that vary. LDA results also depend on the control specifications which include a number of random restarts, iterations, and the specified convergence tolerance, and vary widely with each corpus. In most small business related corpora, we find that acceptable results are generated when control specifications are set for 30 or more random starts, 100,000 iterations, and tolerances are set at 10 -8 . However, in some corpora these numbers are radically lower while in yet other corpora, these values must be quite higher. A quality solution can normally be judged by the probability levels assigned to documents. When the resulting probabilities for the highest correlated documents in the first few topics extracted, approach or exceed 0.9, the result can usually be taken as a very good solution. Probabilities in the 0.5 range and lower, will require adjustments to the control specifications. It is unclear what causes this variability in the solution; however, the variability appears to result as a reflection of the homogeneity in the corpus.

Similar to the application of loading thresholds in LSA, best practices in text mining suppress the reporting of probabilities for terms and documents with low-level probabilities, since they are only peripherally related to the topic. After suppressing low-level probabilities, our LDA results are ready for follow-on analysis.

sections.header_raw: Selecting and preparing topics for monitoring
sections.header_norm: selecting and preparing topics for monitoring
paragraphs: The discussion topics found in text mining results after analyzing customer comments are frequently a mixture of topics that a firm can and cannot influence through its various business functions. Determining which topics from the results should be retained is purely a manual task and highly subjective. The simplest procedure is to attempt to label each extracted topic by reviewing the top five to ten words for each topic. Sometimes a review of the correlated documents is also necessary. Once the topics are labeled, the firm's management can determine which topics they wish to monitor.

The subject matter of topics extracted by LDA and LSA are generally similar; however, we have experienced occasions where approximately 10 % of the extracted topics are semantically very different. Diverging results from the two methods introduce a subjective element in their comparison, especially in exploratory bottom-up studies where the topics are not anticipated. Certain application domains will produce very clean, straightforward semantic spaces and the two methods will extract similar topics. Other domains will involve less clear semantic space which will tend to produce unstable solutions. This is a phenomenon similar to what one might expect to observe when least-squares and maximum likelihood solutions in factor analysis are compared.

With topics of interest determined and defined, we perform simple counts of significant observations for each factor or topic per time period for use in follow-on analysis. In LSA, the number of high loading documents (equivalently, the number of high-probability documents in LDA) for discussion topic k within time period t are

These numbers form the basis for monitoring by some follow-on procedure.

sections.paper_id: Ashton et al. - 2014 - Extending monitoring methods to textual data a research agenda
paragraphs: To illustrate text monitoring we use data from a Fortune 500 retailer that offers an online service to U.S. customers. During a major nationwide marketing campaign, the firm offered trial subscriptions to new customers. At the end of the trial period, customers had the option of either closing their account at no cost or continuing with the firm. When the customer decided to cancel, the firm asked the customer why they were cancelling, and captured their response in text form. We will consider a sample of 8,215 of these customer comments. This service provider is in a highly competitive industry and to protect the firm's identity, various facts are altered. Names for unique services and products that could disclose the identity of the firm or its industry are replaced by a generic yet descriptive term. Where necessary, numbers are also replaced by ratios since many of those numbers appear in public financial records and reports.

Before analysis, we begin with a raw 5,069 terms × 8,215 documents X matrix. Commonly applied data cleaning practices of removing low information words (stopwords), stemming words back to their root terms (Porter stemmer), and removing Arabic numbers are performed as described in Sect. 2. To further reduce the data matrix, sparse terms, terms with 99.75 % (determined by exploration) of their columns having zero frequency count were removed. This resulted in an X matrix with 314 terms × 8,215 documents that is ready for dimensionality testing.

The correct number of dimensions to extract from a term-by-document matrix is an open research question. In IR, the discipline of origin for text mining, there are a number of methods available for determining the correct dimensionality. It is common in IR to extract 200 or more LSA dimensions (Bradford 2008). However, IR is focused on much larger problems, e.g. internet retrieval or mining a large library collection. Researchers who analyzed corpora with a much narrower focus have reported optimal dimensionalities as small as 5 (Lifchitz et al. 2009). We understand that the dimensionality of this data set is relatively small and choose to use a plot of the eigenvalues and select as the dimensions to extract the change point at which the rate of change becomes marginal (see Fig. 3). In this instance, 13 dimensions (discussion topics) are selected as optimal. Note that other change points are also present e.g. 4, 8, and 26 are equally valid dimensionality levels to extract, given that the standard is the presence of a change point. Occasionally, exploration runs with the various dimensionality levels is necessary to find a more meaningful solution to the data.

With dimensionality determined, LDA and LSA algorithms were applied extracting 13 dimensions. The LSA algorithm is an algebraic solution that completed in 14.75 s excluding the X-matrix data preparation. Finding a suitable model with LDA required a number of successive executions. We base our model selection on posterior probabilities with a preference for document and terms that possess probabilities approaching 0.90 in at least some topics. The steps taken and their results for LDA are documented in All operations were performed using an Intel i7-3820 CPU with 32G RAM and the R statistical programming language version 2.15.2 (64 bit) (R Core Team 2012) * The first run was conducted using the default setting for Package Topic Models, an R statistical computing package (Grun and Hornik 2011). Package Topic Models uses 500 iterations with a convergence tolerance of 10 -6 for variational inference and 1,000 iterations and a tolerance of 10 -4 for parameter estimation * * The highest observed term and document probabilities are reported here the model which was ultimately selected for continued analysis. The impact of our configuration setting on the outcome provides some further insights. In previous experiments, a 0.90 document probability standard served us well but in this data set LDA appears incapable of obtaining that standard. Also, we found that topics move about with these solutions meaning that the discussion issue found as topic 13 in run 2 was not in the same position in other solutions. Once the algorithm analysis was complete, the topics were evaluated and defined as described in Sect. 2. We select only one of the topics for presentation here. It focuses on accounts with administrative issues. In this topic, customers claim the account is fraudulent, complain about credit card charges to the account, or are requesting suspension of the account for a period. LSA extracted this as the fifth topic while LDA extracted this it as the 13th topic. Table 2 shows the top 20 terms (LSA) and the top 28 terms (LDA), respectively, for this topic.

During the entire period of data collection, the firm that collected this data set was running a nationwide marketing campaign that offered free trial accounts. That campaign resulted in a continuous increase in accounts as well as a continuous increase in accounts with administrative issues. For a first look at the data, since the customer base is changing over time, we find using time series plots of proportions more revealing than raw numbers. Figures 4 and 5 present plots of weekly observations for the selected topic as a proportion of all observations for that week. The plotted data on these charts is simply the number of observations classified to the topic over the total number of topics in that time frame.

While these plots have some similarity, the difference is explained by the two techniques extracting somewhat different topics. In both instances, the extracted topics discussed customer with fraud accounts, multiple accounts, and accounts with issues because of credit cards charges. A closer look reveals that LDA formed a separate topic for accounts that were suspended for Hurricane Katrina while LSA included those documents with this topic (week 30). However, LDA included a group of documents that simply requested closure at the end of the free trial period. LSA considered that group of documents a separate topic. 0 0.5 1 1.5 2 2.5 3 3.5 0 0.5 1 1.5 2 2.5 3 3.5 Topic 5 Topic 12 Fig. 6 Illustration of overlapping word use in two topics as depicted in vector space by LSA

In Sect. 2, we describe that this phenomenon of different yet similar results occurs because of how closely related the terms are. When words are used across two or more topics, the vectors generated by the algorithm can take different angles through the data. Figure 6 illustrates this phenomenon by using two topics as extracted by LSA. In Fig. 6, the observations in the middle of the field (circled) occur with varying strength (correlation) in both topics. Depending on the angle of approach to this vector space region, certain terms are potentially perceived as a new topic by an algorithm. This overlapping of word usage generates the different interpretations in the results of LDA and LSA. For now, the single best solution appears to use both algorithms and pick between the two sets of results until future research provides further guidance.

We require a method for monitoring these ongoing topics. The objective of monitoring these perceptions is to track conditions within the business silo(s) that generate the observations, which we generally call nonconformities. While a control chart is our choice, there are a number of unresolved issues that arise. These issues we attribute principally to the contrast between the human emotional domain in which text observations arise and the industrial setting in which control charts originate. from a customer comment data set. First, there will be customer centered topics that discuss issues beyond the scope of the business. For example, I am closing my account because I lost my job and need to reduce expenses.

In many instances, these topics are not worth monitoring. However, they are potentially worth passing to a process improvement team for evaluation. In many instances, customers have some common issues that impact their relationship with the firm. On occasion, a creative process improvement team can develop a solution that improves the customer experience with the enterprise and reduces the presence of the topic in future data collection. In the instance of the "…lost my job…" example above, the firm could introduce an option for customers to suspend their account for a period of time.

Then, there are approximately 10-20 recurring service quality-related themes that show up in many corpora. Some of these recurring themes are construct subjects of the classic SERVQUAL, LSQ, or customer satisfaction instruments such as:

• The preferred product was not available • Delivery was slow • Price was high • Product was damaged These are the routine topics that require continual monitoring. In many cases, these topics never disappear completely. However, their proportion of occurrence oscillates with time and they require ongoing diligence. Restated, the observed probability distribution of topics from one time interval to another does not appear fixed; however, we assume that for these topics the larger population distribution is fixed. These observed proportion oscillations are potentially driven by a number of variables but are worthy of ongoing monitoring to avoid an increase in the total number of customer complaints.

Finally, surprise topics can spontaneously emerge within open ended customer feedback. These tend to be environmental (political, economic, or seasonal) but can also be driven by a systemic change in the business. While not absolute, because surprise topics may fade nearly as quickly as they emerge it is critical to monitor and respond as needed to such feedback.

The traditional control charts were designed for mechanized environments. In those environments, the human component was considered; however, it was not the main contributor of the process. The central focus of the classic control chart was a mechanical process. Those control charts can be described as tools for monitoring the response of equipment and processes under human control. However, this paper is concerned with methods for monitoring human responses to the business "machine" or process.

To date, we have explored two control charts with text data in detail. First, the EWMA chart works well in most situations where a one-time solution is required such as a post analysis of a corpus that was assembled over time. The Eigenvectors generated in LSA are severely skewed and the EWMA is robust to non-normality. In a business corpus, there is nearly always a lot of homogeneity and autocorrelation in the data. Homogeneity occurs because one or a limited number of central questions drives the responses. As a result, the business corpora are homogeneous and contain feedback about the same issue. Autocorrelation is caused by the use of key terms by more than one discussion topic.

We have also used the p-chart on a number of corpora. The LSA model of text mining is a linear algebraic solution to the corpus. Using a linear algebra solution, there is a procedure in the LSA model to predict or forecast a solution for new documents given an existing solution. One of the commonly applied procedures is called a fold-in. In experimental studies, we have found that the solutions of a fold-in procedure are not consistent with the results found when the new documents are added to the old and the whole dataset is reanalyzed by LSA. While this might appear an intuitive result we find that using a fold-in is acceptable, but occasionally it is necessary to rerun the analysis on the full data set. The problem with EWMA is that the measurements generated from a fold-in verses full analysis are not mathematically consistent and require a completely new chart build. However, if we express the solution in proportional terms, we can perform fold-in procedures and reanalyze the data on occasion. Another issue that occurs with the EWMA concerns abrupt speakers which are common in social media data. When the dataset is composed of speakers that use abrupt language as well as speakers that use verbose language, the algebraic solution from LSA and the probabilistic solution from LDA will result in lower scores on the documents generated by the abrupt writer. Proportioning the solution and the p-chart eliminates this issue.

sections.paper_id: Ashton et al. - 2014 - Extending monitoring methods to textual data a research agenda
sections.header_raw: Data issues and a research agenda
sections.header_norm: data issues and a research agenda
paragraphs: Before initiating control chart applications to this data set, a number of issues must be addressed.

Which algorithm is best for the given task is not clear. Some comparative testing exists in the literature. Much of that testing has been conducted in IR or CS communities. The data we examine here differs from tradition IR and CS data sets. In the IR and CS communities, the focus is on large datasets e.g. the internet or a very large library. In the IR or CS environment, the dimensionality of a corpus is very high with 200 and more topics (dimensions) in the data. In the business context of customer comments, the dimensionality is much lower because routinely the data originates as a response to a direct-question stimulus or focused set of query terms. Commonly business corpora have 25 or fewer dimensions. No testing exists in the literature comparing algorithm effectiveness under these corpora conditions.

The algorithms have other peculiarities that are not fully explained. In LSA, there is a variety of local and global weighting methods that redistribute word probabilities in the X matrix. Only limited testing of the impact of weightings was performed (Nakov et al. 2001(Nakov et al. , 2003)). That testing, however, was performed with corpora based on novels and news articles. In the business environment, we experience a very different corpus structure; many consist of documents composed with fewer than 50 words. The impact of matrix rotation under diverse corpora characteristics is also not fully understood. Currently with LSA, the main rotation method applied to business corpora is varimax. While varimax has a long-established history in the traditional numerical factor analysis setting, a number of psychometric studies have argued in favor of other rotation procedures, such as oblique rotations (Browne 2001). Further research is needed on LSA rotation choices.

Like LSA, LDA also has issues that require exploration. In our own methods investigations, we have observed LDA to perform successfully with controls set to no random restarts, iterations = 500, and convergence tolerances = 10 -4 , with a certain corpus. Then, with a very similar corpus, a successful performance required pushing the controls out to 30 random restarts, iterations = 150,000, and convergence tolerance = 10 -8 . We consider acceptable solutions as having document probabilities of 0.90 or higher for the first topics extracted. The first instance mentioned above was a corpus generated out of social media by using three diverse query keyword sets. In the second instance, the lower level control settings produced results with probabilities in the 0.4 range. This second data set was also generated out of social media however, the keywords were closely related. Our initial impression is that corpus homogeneity is related to this problem for LDA.

Regarding the corpus, we begin by observing that the nature of the monitored variable is inconsistent with the traditional variables of statistical process control. While we have described the observations as nonconformities, they do not represent nonconforming observations in the sense of the traditional language of quality. Nonconformity is very descriptive in an industrial setting, yet the data we observe is either human opinion or satisfaction expressions and is a behavioral outcome. This behavior outcome is not necessarily evidence of a nonconforming business process. The business process may be working as designed, while the customer prefers something else. Therefore, something might be out of control, just not the silo nearest the complaining customer. Also note that nonconformity implies a negative result. We have observed the extraction of fully positive sentiment topics as well as a mix of varying degrees of positive and negative expressions within a single topic. Many factors can drive customer behavior. While beyond the scope of this research, the customer behavioral response is potentially driven by perceived goodness-of-fit, the result of an opinion leader, or a competitor launching a new marketing campaign. For now, we retain the traditional "nonconformity" label until a more appropriate term emerges.

Business corpora are very different from traditional information retrieval corpora in other ways as well. Because the business corpora are typically collected in response to a question, the resulting discussion topics found in the business corpus tend to have a homogeneous nature. This homogeneity appears to influence analytic results. The algorithms have difficulty separating closely related topics due to overlap in word usage. Homogeneity in the corpus however, does not necessarily extend to data collected from a social media source. Social media data while routinely possessing a homogeneous nature is subject to uncontrollable information events, announcements from news media, and political entities events. These information event sources act like systemic shocks and cause severe oscillations in the data that may not reflect ground-truth. How to moderate these shocks is not clear.

Like social media sourced data, business text data is also subject to shock. The observed shock does not necessarily originate from the business process to which the discussion topics refer. The origin of the shock is attributable to such actions as the firm's promotion efforts, nature, news media surrounding the firm or the firm's industry and competitors. As observed in the illustration study, a shock occurred because of hurricane Katrina during week 30. Data collected from social media outlets is particularly subject to shock because it has a voluntary nature and the respondents usually have an emotional reason to engage the conversation. While shocks are not unusual for business, how we detect and address automatic handling of system shocks in text data is not clear.

Many business corpora have a time component to them and experience considerable turbulence over time. The opinion constantly moves positively and negatively in response not only to the firm and its product, but also due to nature, politics, economics, competitor actions, and opinion leaders. Because of these influences and shocks, the observed system is almost never stable. As time moves, new information becomes available, and customer opinion changes. Turbulence in the data becomes even larger as the industry becomes more competitive or as the firm's strategy becomes more offensive.

The sentiment of the expressions changes over time and is potentially positive and negative simultaneously in a single corpus. Mixed sentiment data is common in the corpus originating within the business, but is even more prevalent in corpora originating from a social network source. Two approaches are available to the researcher: keep all observations in a single data set and view it as a measure of the total strength of the underlying discussion topic; or split the data into positive, negative, and neutral sentiment subsets. Splitting data along sentiment lines is typically performed by dictionaries. Some corpora will require data splitting prior to processing while in other instances, the data for a specific topic is split after text mining.

As illustrated toward the end of Sect. 3, slight variations occur in the solutions of the two analytic methods. These variations appear as the result of how the algorithms view and allocate the vector space. While for some it is preferable to apply only one algorithm, a more prudent response is to run both algorithms until a more advanced and resolutely superior algorithm emerges.

Many of the aforementioned issues are relatively straightforward in their solution. Determining the effect of document size, corpus size, and homogeneity on the solutions generated by the algorithms is a matter of systematic rigorous repeated testing. This repeated testing also needs to address the variations in analytic solutions and generalizable solutions to mixed sentiment data. Because of the variation in solutions, we recommend conducting exploration runs of new corpora with both algorithms followed by a rigorous review of the results.

How to address shock and time components of text data is not clear. The shocks that a business system can endure originates from a variety of places. When the origin is internal, then the firm has the opportunity to address the problem and move forward. Product recalls by firms are a good example of this. When a design issue emerges in a product, it can cause either a sudden spike or a slow rise over time in the volume of internally collected data. Then when the recall is issued, a second spike can appear in social media data as customers, news media, and potential customers express their opinion of events. From a control charting perspective, this type of shock appears fairly straightforward-fix the business process, the problem goes away, and the data returns to the mean. However, that is not always the case because the expressions reflect behavior that is opinion driven. In a traditional SPC environment, once the system was repaired, a new data collection period or phase in the control chart is initiated with the process setting a new mean. Customer expression data may return to the mean immediately, slowly, or establish a new mean after a shock-causing event has corrected. In some cases residual effects of opinion driven behavior responses can linger for extended periods exerting pressure on the mean of the data for an indeterminate period while the hangover effect fades.

Many of the aforementioned issues are extensively researched in retail marketing; however, how these effects should be addressed in an ongoing monitoring apparatus is unclear. One possible consideration is to introduce a new class of SPC control chart with relaxed interpretation rules for monitoring behavioral data. Alternately, we could stay within the existing SPC framework and argue for a new set of chart interpretation rules for behavioral data.

tags.reason: Discusses data issues, challenges, and outlines a research agenda, focusing on interpretation and fu

sections.paper_id: Ashton et al. - 2014 - Extending monitoring methods to textual data a research agenda
paragraphs: LSA and LDA represent two widely accepted methods of text mining. Testing of these methods against corpora originating in business and business oriented social media is limited. Because of this limited testing, which method is most appropriate for a given corpus structure is unknown. Future testing needs to consider information homogeneity within the corpora, document length, and the number of documents in the corpora. Additionally, while some rules of thumb are available, a clear recipe does not exist for selecting among the many weighting schemes and matrix rotation methods used in LSA. The text mining community is best served by a systematic, experimentally designed approach to test algorithms and to solving these issues. Until these issues are resolved, the researcher is best advised to conduct exploratory analysis using multiple techniques with any new data set.

Further, while the ability to automatically extract meaningful information from large collections of customer data exists, a number of issues prevent the casual loading of those results to existing monitoring tools. The complications begin with the very definition of the variable data; is it, or is it not a non-conformity? While this is might appear a trivial issue because the rules of SPC control charts are well defined it is not as clear when the movement of data follows an emotional response. Communicating these differences across research communities is interesting and important because human behavior refuses to follow the expectations of industrial standards.
